expert_identity.name: Local GCN (1-hop)
expert_identity.summary: First-order graph convolution aggregating information from immediate graph neighbors (as defined by the functional-connectivity adjacency).
expert_identity.mechanism: 1-hop message passing using a first-order spectral approximation (e.g., renormalized Laplacian / first-order Chebyshev-style formulation as in standard GCN).
strengths: Captures tightly coupled connectivity patterns within locally connected graph neighborhoods.
Often robust for modular subgraphs with strong within-module coherence.
expert_identity.description: E1 aggregates only over immediate graph neighbors (i.e., edges in the adjacency matrix derived from functional connectivity, not necessarily spatial adjacency). This is often a good fit for SMN (sensorimotor areas with strong within-network coupling) and VN (visual occipital regions with strong within-network coupling).

expert_identity.name: Two-layer GCN (2-hop receptive field)
expert_identity.summary: A mid-range graph model capturing direct and indirect pathways via a 2-hop receptive field.
expert_identity.mechanism: A 2-hop receptive field achieved by stacking two 1-hop GCN layers (or equivalently using a K=2 polynomial filter, depending on implementation).
strengths: Balances local specialization with moderate cross-module integration.
Captures indirect functional dependencies through short graph paths.
expert_identity.description: E2 captures short-to-mid range dependencies via 2-hop propagation. This can suit DAN (dorsal frontoparietal attention system), VAN (ventral attention/salience network involving insula and midcingulate cortex), LIN (limbic/paralimbic affective circuitry including orbitofrontal, parahippocampal, and temporal pole regions), and SBN (subcortical nuclei such as thalamus, basal ganglia, hippocampus, and amygdala interacting with cortex via multi-synaptic loops).

expert_identity.name: Graph Transformer
expert_identity.summary: Global self-attention dynamically weighting pairwise node interactions to model long-range integration.
expert_identity.mechanism: Multi-head self-attention over node embeddings augmented with graph structural positional encodings (e.g., Laplacian eigenvector PE or random-walk structural encodings).
strengths: Models long-distance functional coupling between spatially remote regions.
Can highlight hub nodes and cross-network integration patterns.
expert_identity.description: E3 supports flexible long-range integration. This is often useful for DMN (distributed medial prefrontal, posterior cingulate, angular gyrus, and temporal regions) and FPN (distributed lateral prefrontal and parietal regions coordinating control and coupling across multiple systems).

expert_identity.name: Spectral GNN (multi-scale filtering)
expert_identity.summary: Spectral-domain graph filtering capturing multi-scale topological structure through frequency-aware transformations of graph signals.
expert_identity.mechanism: Learnable spectral filters defined in the Laplacian spectrum, commonly implemented via polynomial spectral approximations (e.g., Chebyshev polynomials) and optionally via explicit eigenbasis methods when feasible.
strengths: Captures smooth (low-frequency) and localized (high-frequency) components of graph signals.
Sensitive to community structure, modularity, and hierarchical organization.
expert_identity.description: E4 decomposes connectivity patterns into frequency components, enabling simultaneous characterization of global integration and fine-grained modular structure. This can be useful for CBL (cerebellar organization with lobular structure and long-range cerebro-cerebellar loops) and can complement attention-based global models on DMN and FPN by emphasizing hierarchical modularity in the spectral domain.

knowledge_summary.summary: Individuals with ADHD frequently show excessive activation and reduced task-related suppression in Default Mode Network (DMN) hubs, termed 'DMN interference,' which is linked to attentional lapses.
claims: DMN often fails to deactivate properly during attention-demanding tasks.
Precuneus and mPFC frequently exhibit persistent hyperconnectivity.
Aberrant DMNâ€“FPN anticorrelation is a robust predictor of inattentive symptoms.

knowledge_summary.summary: Weakened long-range connectivity between the Frontoparietal Control Network (FPN) and Dorsal Attention Network (DAN) is often observed, contributing to deficits in top-down attentional control.
claims: Reduced intra-FPN connectivity and diminished coupling with DAN are common findings.
Impaired top-down control affects the ability to shift attention voluntarily.
Hypoconnectivity in these networks correlates with the severity of clinical inattention.

knowledge_summary.title: Motor and cerebellar network dysfunction
knowledge_summary.summary: Abnormal connectivity in somatomotor and cerebellar networks reflects deficits in motor control and temporal processing (timing), which are frequent endophenotypes of ADHD.
claims: Reduced Precentralâ€“Cerebellar coupling impairs sensorimotor integration.
Hyperconnectivity within SMN is often linked to hyperactivity/impulsivity.
Cerebellar hypoactivation is associated with deficits in interval timing tasks.

knowledge_summary.summary: Dysfunction in the Salience Network (AIâ€“ACC) can disrupt the 'switching' mechanism between DMN and FPN, contributing to impaired response inhibition.
claims: Reduced connectivity between Anterior Insula (AI) and ACC decreases salience detection.
Diminished SNâ€“FPN coupling impairs engagement with task-relevant stimuli.
Abnormal insula activation correlates with impulsivity scores in many cohorts.

knowledge_summary.summary: Longitudinal studies suggest a pattern of 'delayed maturation' in ADHD, particularly in frontostriatal circuits and the segregation of DMNâ€“FPN systems.
claims: Frontostriatal connectivity tends to increase more slowly with age compared to controls.
DMNâ€“FPN anticorrelation often reaches mature levels later than in typical development.
Delayed network segregation provides a neural basis for persistent symptoms into adolescence.

knowledge_summary.summary: Individuals with ADHD who have higher IQs often exhibit partial normalization or compensatory hyperconnectivity in prefrontal and parietal regions, supporting better cognitive control.
claims: Enhanced DLPFCâ€“Parietal coupling is observed in high-IQ ADHD groups.
Compensatory activation may mitigate observed attentional lapses.
Cognitive reserve may mask underlying connectivity deficits in behavioral performance.

problem_prompt.instruction: When this demographic information and medical background knowledge appear, which type of expert is more suitable for each node?
